question.text: Harish, a Hindu man, makes a valid will in 2020 and subsequently marries Pooja in 2022. He does not make a new will after his marriage. What happens to his 2020 will under the Indian Succession Act?

answer.text: The will is automatically revoked because marriage always revokes a prior will
The will is partially revoked — only the spouse's share is affected
The will remains valid because under Section 57, marriage does NOT revoke a will for Hindus
The will is suspended until Harish either confirms or revokes it after marriage

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under Section 57 of the Indian Succession Act, subsequent marriage does NOT revoke a will for Hindus. This is a specific exception. For non-Hindus, subsequent marriage IS one of the 4 modes of revocation. The 4 modes are: (1) later will/codicil, (2) written declaration, (3) burning/tearing/destroying, (4) subsequent marriage (but NOT for Hindus). **The Trap:** The general rule is that subsequent marriage revokes a will, but Hindu law has a specific exception under Sec 57. Candidates who know the general rule but not the Hindu exception will get this wrong. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM X-B tests will revocation modes. Also know that a Codicil (Sec 2(b)) is a supplementary document for minor alterations to a will. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always ask Hindu clients whether they've married after making their will. While the will stays valid, it may not reflect their new family situation — updating it is strongly recommended.
